concerns.text: FR-005 and FR-006 prescribe a linear mixed-effects model with 'year' as a fixed effect and 'genre' as a random intercept, applied to 'yearly_similarity.csv'. However, the data unit in this CSV is a single scalar (mean off-diagonal similarity) per year. A mixed-effects model requires multiple observations per level of the random effect (genre) to estimate variance components. With only one data point per year, the model is mathematically degenerate (perfect collinearity between year and the observation). The design conflates a time-series trend test (which requires a single series of yearly means) with a mixed-effects design (which requires cross-sectional variation within years). This renders the statistical test invalid.

FR-004 aggregates track-level data into a single 'mean off-diagonal similarity' per year. FR-005 then attempts to model this aggregated mean against 'year'. This is a classic ecological fallacy / aggregation bias risk. The 'genre' random effect in FR-005 cannot be estimated because the aggregation step (FR-004) has already collapsed the genre-level variation into a single scalar per year. The model cannot distinguish between 'genre variance' and 'year variance' because the data structure no longer supports it. The validation target (slope significance) is derived from a model that cannot be fitted to the constructed data.

FR-006 mandates Bonferroni correction 'across the set of yearly similarity estimates'. Bonferroni correction is used for multiple hypothesis testing (e.g., testing 20 different hypotheses). Here, there is only one hypothesis being tested: 'Is the slope of similarity over time non-zero?'. Applying Bonferroni to a single regression coefficient is statistically nonsensical and indicates a misunderstanding of the correction's purpose. If the intent was to test pairwise differences between years, the model specification in FR-005 is insufficient.

SC-002 requires a 'statistically significant year-slope' as a success criterion. This pre-asserts a specific empirical outcome (a trend exists) as a requirement for the project's success. In scientific research, a null result (no trend) is a valid and publishable finding. Framing the project's success as contingent on finding a significant trend introduces confirmation bias and violates the principle of non-triviality; the project is designed to 'prove' a hypothesis rather than test it.
